notes: Design-review evals targeting two real-use weak spots: (1) sense of overall terminal clutter/density, (2) responsive behavior across terminal sizes. Eval 1 = explicit clutter review with a busy mockup. Eval 2 = explicit responsive question. Eval 3 = open-ended review that names NEITHER concern, to test whether the skill surfaces clutter + responsiveness proactively.

evals.name: cluttered-ci-dashboard-review
assertions: Names the nested / redundant borders (boxes inside boxes) as a specific clutter source
Recommends replacing borders with whitespace for at least one region
Flags the redundant decoration (e.g. the duplicated check-mark around PASSED, or version in the border)
Proactively addresses behavior at narrower terminal widths even though the prompt only asked about clutter

evals.name: responsive-miller-columns
evals.prompt: I've built a 3-pane Ratatui file-manager layout — Miller columns: parent | current | preview — and it looks great on my 220-column ultrawide. The problem is our users run it over SSH, inside tmux splits, and some on 13-inch laptops. Right now it just renders all three columns at 33% width no matter what. Below about 90 columns the preview pane is unreadable and filenames truncate to mush; at 80x24 it's basically unusable, and I haven't even thought about what happens narrower than that. What do I need to do to make this hold up across terminal sizes — 80x24, tmux splits, even down around 60 columns?
assertions: Specifies concrete width breakpoints / thresholds (named column counts)
Recommends collapsing to fewer panes (single-pane) at narrow widths
Recommends a pane priority — e.g. drop the preview first
Mentions a minimum terminal size AND a 'too small' fallback message
Recommends percentage / min-width / constraint-based layout instead of fixed 33%

evals.name: open-ended-k8s-tui-review
evals.prompt:
Here's the design for a new Kubernetes TUI I'm about to build in Rust with Ratatui, modeled loosely on k9s. I'd love a UX review before I write any code.

It's a 4-panel grid. Top-left is a namespace list, top-right is a pod list. Bottom-left is a pod details panel that shows every field — name, status, pod IP, host IP, node, all labels, all annotations, restart count, age, QoS class, conditions, and the owner reference. Bottom-right is a live log tail. Every panel has a rounded border with a title in it. There's a header bar across the top showing cluster / context / namespace, and a footer bar that lists all 14 keybindings. Color coding: green = running, red = failed/crashloop, yellow = pending. The whole thing is designed for my 160x50 terminal window.

What do you think — anything you'd change before I start?
assertions: Proactively flags the 'every field' details panel as clutter and recommends prioritization or detail-on-Enter
Proactively flags the 14-key footer as a wall-of-shortcuts and recommends showing a few + ? for the rest
Proactively raises responsiveness (what happens below 160x50) though the prompt never mentions size
Flags color-only status coding (green/red/yellow) and recommends pairing with symbols or letters
